label: human
author: Christophe Buffet, École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales (EHESS)
text:
Initially championed by small island states (AOSIS), the push to include the 1.5°C target more prominently in the Paris agreement achieved surprising progress. While the 2°C global temperature limitation goal was adopted with difficulty at Copenhagen in 2009, demanding increased ambition seemed unrealistic to many. This issue represents a scientifically and politically characteristic node within the climate regime.

Numerous studies demonstrate that the 2°C target itself is becoming unreachable, as it would require extremely profound economic and social transformations starting today. However, national commitments submitted for COP — the "nationally determined contributions" or NDCs — revealed this radical trajectory was not being followed. Even if these commitments were genuinely implemented afterward, they would induce global warming estimated by the UNFCCC at minimum 2.7°C.

Politically, the 1.5°C figure is sensitive. The AOSIS group alone carried this demand at Copenhagen. Even NGOs (particularly the Climate Action Network coalition) had previously agreed on 2°C and celebrated its inclusion in Copenhagen's accord as one of the rare positive, even historic, Summit elements. Before COP21, countries considered "most vulnerable," now supported by NGOs, hardened their position and demanded clear reference to this 1.5°C "threshold" as a necessary condition for any agreement, encountering resistance from developed countries and emerging economies alike.

The paradox was observing this figure's progression in the political arena while conditions for its concrete implementation seemed even less achievable than at Copenhagen. According to certain climatologists, it is already exceeded due to current atmospheric greenhouse gas concentrations. Do negotiators forget the science? What then is the sense of inscribing a stricter 1.5°C global temperature limitation objective?
